original_abstract: US government research into the health consequences of radiation exposure from nuclear weaponry involved egregious violations of the human rights of the Marshallese people. Evidence suggests that radioactive fallout from Pacific testing likely reached other Micronesian islands as well. To address this legacy, the US government must declassify all archived data regarding the history of these tests, their atmospheric fallout patterns, and the subsequent health impacts on populations across Micronesia. Furthermore, the global health community must advocate for nuclear-armed states to honor their treaty obligations and pursue the total elimination of nuclear weapons. Finally, as a matter of social justice and a commitment to the fundamental right to health, the federal government should expand Medicaid eligibility to include all citizens of the Compact of Free Association nations.
humanized_abstract: On Oct. 30, 1962, the U.S. conducted the largest nuclear bomb explosion in history, on the atoll of Namu in what is now the Marshall Islands. Testing a 15-megaton hydrogen bomb left the Marshall Islands awash in radiation and marked the start of a program of research into the health damage caused by radiation from nuclear weapons. The Marshall Islanders had their human rights violated in the name of this research. It is now believed that radioactive fallout from Pacific tests also drifted to other islands in Micronesia. The US must release all classified information it has concerning the cold war nuclear tests, the air drift of the fallout and the effects of ionizing radiation on the populations of Micronesia. We urge the international health community to call on nuclear powers to act on their obligations under the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons with the objective of achieving the total elimination of nuclear weapons. We urge the US government to act on justice and basic human rights principles by expanding Medicaid to cover all citizens of Compact of Free Association nations.
